questions.explanation: The `-c` flag in `git switch -c <branch-name>` stands for 'create'. This flag tells Git to create a new branch with the specified name and immediately switch to it. This is equivalent to running `git branch <branch-name>` followed by `git switch <branch-name>`.

questions.question_text:
What does the following `git status -s` output indicate?

```
 M config.json
```
options: A. The file is modified and staged for commit
B. The file is modified in the working directory but NOT staged
C. The file is a new untracked file
D. The file has been deleted from the working directory
questions.explanation: In `git status -s` output, each line has two columns: the first represents the staging area status, and the second represents the working directory status. When there's a space in the first column and 'M' in the second column (` M`), it means the file has been modified in the working directory but has NOT been staged yet. If it were staged, the 'M' would appear in the first column instead.

questions.question_text: What does the `git status --ignored` command do?
options: A) Removes ignored files from the repository
B) Shows files that are hidden by .gitignore rules
C) Creates a new .gitignore file
D) Ignores all untracked files permanently
questions.explanation: By default, `git status` hides files that match patterns in .gitignore. The `--ignored` flag reveals these hidden files, allowing you to see what is being excluded from version control. This is useful for verifying your .gitignore is working correctly.

questions.question_text: What does `git show` display by default when run without any arguments?
options: A. A list of all commits in the repository
B. The last commit with its full diff
C. The current working directory status
D. All tags in the repository
questions.explanation: When `git show` is run without arguments, it defaults to showing the last commit (HEAD), including the commit hash, author, date, commit message, and the full diff of all changes made in that commit. This is different from `git log` which lists multiple commits, or `git status` which shows the working directory state.

questions.question_text: What is the difference between `git remote -v` and `git remote` (without flags)?
options: A) There is no difference; both commands show the same output
B) `git remote -v` shows remote names with their fetch and push URLs, while `git remote` only shows remote names
C) `git remote -v` shows verbose error messages, while `git remote` runs silently
D) `git remote -v` validates the remote URLs, while `git remote` just lists them
questions.explanation: The `-v` flag stands for 'verbose' and causes `git remote` to display the URLs associated with each remote for both fetch and push operations. Without the flag, `git remote` only lists the short names of the configured remotes (like 'origin' or 'upstream').

questions.question_text: What does the reference `HEAD@{1}` point to in Git?
options: A) The parent commit of the current HEAD
B) The position of HEAD one action ago in the reflog
C) The first commit in the repository
D) The commit from one day ago
questions.explanation: HEAD@{1} uses reflog reference syntax to point to where HEAD was one action ago, not one commit ago. This is different from HEAD~1 or HEAD^1, which reference parent commits. The reflog tracks actions like commits, resets, checkouts, and merges, so HEAD@{1} refers to HEAD's position before the most recent action was performed.

questions.question_text: What does Git reflog primarily track?
options: A. The commit history that is shared with remote repositories
B. Every time HEAD moves in your local repository
C. File changes between commits
D. Branch merge conflicts
questions.explanation: Git reflog (reference log) is Git's local diary that records every time HEAD moves in your repository. Unlike git log which shows commit history, reflog tracks your actions including commits, resets, checkouts, rebases, merges, and cherry-picks. It's local only and never shared with remote repositories.

questions.question_text: What command do you use to stop a rebase that is currently in progress and return to the original state?
options: A. git rebase --stop
B. git rebase --abort
C. git rebase --cancel
D. git rebase --undo
questions.explanation: The correct command is `git rebase --abort`. This command stops the rebase process immediately and restores your branch to exactly the state it was in before you started the rebase. The other options are not valid Git commands.

questions.question_text: What does the Git range notation `A..B` represent?
options: A) All commits between A and B inclusive
B) Commits reachable from B that are not reachable from A
C) Commits reachable from either A or B, but not both
D) The common ancestor of A and B
questions.explanation: The double dot notation `A..B` means 'commits reachable from B that are not reachable from A'. Think of it as asking 'what does B have that A doesn't?' This is commonly used to see what commits exist on a feature branch that haven't been merged into main yet.

questions.question_text: What is the main difference between `git pull` and `git pull --rebase`?
options: A) git pull --rebase is faster than regular git pull
B) git pull creates a merge commit, while git pull --rebase replays local commits on top of remote changes
C) git pull --rebase only fetches changes without applying them
D) git pull --rebase automatically resolves all merge conflicts
questions.explanation: Regular `git pull` fetches remote changes and creates a merge commit to combine them with your local commits. In contrast, `git pull --rebase` fetches remote changes and then replays your local commits on top of them, resulting in a linear history without merge commits. This makes the commit history cleaner and easier to read.

questions.question_text: What does Git LFS store in your main Git repository instead of the actual large file content?
options: A. A compressed version of the binary file
B. A small pointer file containing a text reference with a hash
C. A thumbnail preview of the file
D. The file's metadata and permissions only
questions.explanation: Git LFS replaces large files in your repository with small pointer files that contain a text reference and a hash. The actual file content is stored on a separate LFS server and fetched on demand when you clone or checkout. This keeps the main repository lightweight while still allowing version control of large binary files.

questions.question_text: What happens to your local branches and working directory when you run `git fetch --all`?
options: A. Local branches are updated to match remote branches, but the working directory stays the same
B. Both local branches and the working directory are updated with the latest remote changes
C. Neither local branches nor the working directory are changed; only remote-tracking branches are updated
D. The working directory is updated, but local branches remain unchanged
questions.explanation: The key characteristic of `git fetch --all` is that it only downloads information and updates remote-tracking branches (like origin/main). Your local branches (like main) and your working directory remain completely untouched. This is what makes it a 'safe' command - it's like checking your mailbox without opening any letters.

questions.question_text: What information does `git diff --stat` display?
options: A. The full content of all changed files
B. A summary showing which files changed and lines added/removed per file
C. Only the names of files that were deleted
D. The commit history with author information
questions.explanation: The `git diff --stat` command provides a high-level summary of changes, showing which files changed, how many lines were added/removed per file (with a visual bar representation), and a total summary at the bottom. It does not show the full content of changes like the regular `git diff` command.

questions.question_text: What does the `git diff --name-only` command output?
options: A. The full diff content showing line-by-line changes
B. Only the filenames that have changed
C. A summary of insertions and deletions per file
D. The commit hashes where files were modified
questions.explanation: The `--name-only` flag modifies `git diff` to output only the names of files that have changed, without showing the actual content of the changes. This produces a clean list of filenames, one per line, which is useful for scripting and quick scanning.

questions.question_text: What is the correct command to remove a Git configuration entry for user.email from the global config file?
options: A. git config --delete --global user.email
B. git config --global --unset user.email
C. git config --remove user.email --global
D. git config --global user.email --clear
questions.explanation: The correct syntax is `git config --global --unset user.email`. The `--unset` flag removes a configuration entry, and `--global` specifies the scope (the ~/.gitconfig file). Options A, C, and D use incorrect flags that don't exist in Git's config command.

questions.question_text: You just committed your changes but realized you forgot to include the file `utils.js`. What is the correct sequence of commands to add this file to your most recent commit without changing the commit message?
options: A. git commit --amend utils.js
B. git add utils.js && git commit --amend --no-edit
C. git amend --add utils.js
D. git add utils.js && git commit --amend -m "same message"
questions.explanation: To add a forgotten file to the most recent commit, you first need to stage the file using `git add utils.js`, then run `git commit --amend --no-edit`. The `--no-edit` flag tells Git to keep the original commit message unchanged. Option A is incorrect because --amend doesn't accept file arguments directly. Option C uses invalid syntax. Option D would work but requires you to retype the message, which is unnecessary and error-prone.

questions.question_text: Before running `git clean -fd` to remove untracked files and directories, what command should you run first to safely preview what will be deleted?
questions.explanation: The -n or --dry-run flag performs a preview of what would be deleted without actually removing anything. Running `git clean -fd -n` shows you exactly which files and directories would be removed, allowing you to verify before executing the destructive operation. This is critical because git clean permanently deletes files that are NOT in Git history and cannot be recovered.

questions.question_text: What is the primary purpose of the `git cherry-pick` command?
options: A) To merge an entire branch into the current branch
B) To apply a specific commit from one branch to your current branch as a new commit
C) To delete a commit from the repository history
D) To create a new branch from a specific commit
questions.explanation: git cherry-pick is designed to take a specific commit from anywhere in your repository and apply it to your current branch as a new commit. Unlike merging, which brings in all commits from a branch, cherry-pick allows you to selectively apply individual commits. This is useful for applying hotfixes across branches or pulling in specific features without a full merge.

questions.question_text:
You're reviewing `auth.js` and find a suspicious line of code. You want to find out WHO wrote that specific line and in WHICH commit it was introduced.

Which command shows the author and commit information for each line in a file?
options: A. git log auth.js
B. git blame auth.js
C. git show auth.js
D. git diff auth.js
questions.explanation: git blame shows line-by-line annotation of a file, displaying who last modified each line, which commit introduced it, and when it was changed. git log shows commit history but doesn't break it down by individual lines. git show displays commit details, and git diff shows differences between commits or working directory.

questions.question_text:
You modified app.js with multiple changes, but you only want to stage SOME of the changes (not the entire file).

Which command lets you interactively choose which parts (hunks) of a file to stage?
options: git add app.js --partial
git add -p app.js
git stage --interactive app.js
git commit --patch app.js
questions.explanation: git add -p (or --patch) lets you interactively select which changes to stage, one 'hunk' at a time. Option A is invalid as --partial is not a valid flag. Option C uses --interactive which works differently (menu-based, not hunk-by-hunk). Option D is invalid because committing doesn't work with --patch in this way.

questions.question_text: A developer notices their Git repository is tracking `__pycache__/` directories and `.pyc` files. What do these files represent, and why should they be included in `.gitignore`?
questions.correct_answer: These are Python compiled bytecode files. `__pycache__/` directories contain cached `.pyc` (compiled Python) and `.pyo` (optimized bytecode) files that Python generates automatically when running code. They should be gitignored because they are machine-generated, platform-specific, can be regenerated from source files, and would create unnecessary noise in version control.
questions.explanation: Python automatically compiles source code to bytecode for faster execution. These files are regenerated as needed and vary between Python versions and platforms, making them unsuitable for version control. Including them would bloat the repository and cause merge conflicts.

questions.question_text:
Which of the following scenarios will cause Git to pause during a rebase, making `git rebase --continue` a valid next step?

1. A merge conflict occurs
2. A commit is marked with `edit` in interactive rebase
3. A `break` command is encountered in the todo list
4. The rebase completes successfully
options: A. 1 and 2 only
B. 1, 2, and 3 only
C. 2 and 3 only
D. All of the above (1, 2, 3, and 4)
questions.explanation: Git pauses a rebase in three situations: (1) after a merge conflict that needs resolution, (2) after applying a commit marked with `edit` in interactive rebase, and (3) when encountering a `break` command in the todo list. When a rebase completes successfully, there is no pause—the operation is finished, so `--continue` would not be valid.

questions.question_text: You are performing an interactive rebase and marked a commit with `edit` to amend it. After making your changes, you try to continue but receive an error. Describe the correct sequence of commands to successfully continue the rebase after editing a commit.
questions.correct_answer: After making changes, you must stage the modified files using `git add <files>` (or `git add .` for all changes) before running `git rebase --continue`. The complete sequence is: make your changes, run `git add <files>`, then run `git rebase --continue`.
questions.explanation: Git requires that all changes be staged before continuing a paused rebase. This applies whether you're resolving merge conflicts or editing commits during an interactive rebase. If you have unstaged changes or unresolved conflicts, Git will remind you to address them before allowing the rebase to continue. This ensures that Git knows exactly what changes should be included in the rebased commit.

questions.question_text:
What is the correct order of the three main areas in Git's architecture, from where you edit files to where permanent history is stored?

A. Index → Working directory → Object database
B. Object database → Index → Working directory
C. Working directory → Index → Object database
D. Working directory → Object database → Index
options: A. Index → Working directory → Object database
B. Object database → Index → Working directory
C. Working directory → Index → Object database
D. Working directory → Object database → Index
questions.explanation: The correct flow is: Working directory (where you edit files) → Index/staging area (where you stage changes with `git add`) → Object database (where commits are permanently stored). When you run `git add`, changes move from working directory to index. When you run `git commit`, Git builds a tree from the index and stores it in the object database.

questions.question_text:
Consider the following Git tree structure:

```
tree 8a3f2b...
├── README.md      -> blob 4c1a9e...
├── main.py        -> blob 7d2f8c...
└── src/           -> tree 2b9e4a...
    └── utils.py   -> blob 1f3d7b...
```

If you modify only `utils.py` and create a new commit, which objects will Git need to create new SHA-1 hashes for?
options: A) Only the blob for utils.py
B) The blob for utils.py, the src/ tree, and the root tree
C) All blobs and all trees in the repository
D) The blob for utils.py and the src/ tree only
questions.explanation: When utils.py changes, Git creates a new blob with the new content. Since the src/ tree now points to a different blob, it gets a new SHA-1 hash. The root tree must also change because it now points to a different src/ tree. However, README.md and main.py blobs remain unchanged and are reused. This cascading update from the changed file up to the root is how Git tracks changes efficiently while avoiding duplication of unchanged content.

questions.question_text: You're trying to switch branches with `git checkout feature-branch` but Git refuses with a message about your working tree. You have some experimental changes you want to keep but aren't ready to commit. What is the best approach to resolve this?
options: A. Run `git checkout -- .` to clear the working tree
B. Run `git stash` to temporarily save changes, then switch branches
C. Run `git reset --hard HEAD` to force the checkout
D. Delete the local repository and clone it again
questions.explanation: Using `git stash` is the best approach when you have changes you want to keep but aren't ready to commit. It temporarily saves your modifications and gives you a clean working tree, allowing you to switch branches. Later, you can retrieve your changes with `git stash pop`. Option A (`git checkout -- .`) would discard your changes permanently. Options C and D are destructive approaches that would also lose your work.

questions.question_text: Explain the three different ways to resolve a dirty working tree in Git, and describe a scenario where each approach would be most appropriate.
questions.correct_answer: The three ways to resolve a dirty working tree are: 1) Commit the changes (`git add . && git commit -m "message"`) - best when changes are complete and ready to be saved permanently; 2) Stash the changes (`git stash`) - best when you need to temporarily set aside work-in-progress to do something else, like switch branches or pull updates; 3) Discard the changes (`git checkout -- .`) - best when the changes were experimental and you've decided you don't want to keep them. Each approach serves a different purpose depending on whether you want to preserve the changes permanently, temporarily, or not at all.
questions.explanation: Understanding when to use each method is crucial for effective Git workflow. Committing is for finished work you want in your history. Stashing is for work-in-progress you need to set aside temporarily (like when you need to quickly fix a bug on another branch). Discarding is for changes you've decided against keeping. Using the wrong approach can lead to lost work (discarding when you meant to stash) or cluttered history (committing half-finished work).

questions.question_text: A developer tries to push their commits but receives a rejection message saying their branch is "behind" the remote. What is the correct explanation for this rejection?
options: A. The developer doesn't have write permission to the repository
B. The remote has commits that the developer doesn't have locally, requiring a pull/merge first
C. The remote server is temporarily unavailable
D. Branch protection rules are blocking direct pushes
questions.explanation: When Git says your branch is 'behind' the remote, it means the remote repository contains commits that you don't have in your local repository. This typically happens when someone else has pushed changes to the same branch. Git rejects the push to prevent you from accidentally overwriting their work. You need to pull and merge (or rebase) the remote changes first, then push again.

questions.question_text:
Given the following directory structure, which path would contain the remote-tracking branch for `origin/feature-x`?

```
.git/refs/
├── heads/
├── remotes/
│   └── origin/
└── tags/
```
options: A. .git/refs/heads/origin/feature-x
B. .git/refs/remotes/feature-x
C. .git/refs/remotes/origin/feature-x
D. .git/refs/tags/origin/feature-x
questions.explanation: Remote-tracking branches are stored under `.git/refs/remotes/`. The remote name (in this case `origin`) creates a subdirectory, and the branch name (`feature-x`) is a file within that subdirectory. So the full path is `.git/refs/remotes/origin/feature-x`. Local branches go in `refs/heads/` and tags go in `refs/tags/`.

questions.question_text: You want to list all untracked files in your Git repository, but exclude files that match patterns in your `.gitignore`. Write the complete Git command to accomplish this.
questions.explanation: The `--others` flag shows untracked files (files not in the index). The `--exclude-standard` flag applies the standard Git exclusion rules, which include patterns from `.gitignore`, `.git/info/exclude`, and the global gitignore file. Combining these flags gives you untracked files that aren't being intentionally ignored.

questions.question_text:
Consider this scenario: You ran `git fetch` on Monday at 9am. Your teammate pushed 5 commits to the `main` branch on Monday at 3pm and created a new branch called `feature-login`. On Tuesday morning, you run `git log origin/main --oneline -n 3` without fetching first.

What will you see?
options: A) The 3 most recent commits including your teammate's work
B) The 3 most recent commits from Monday 9am's state, missing your teammate's commits
C) An error because the remote has changed
D) The feature-login branch commits
questions.explanation: Remote-tracking branches only update when you explicitly run git fetch. Between fetches, they are 'frozen in time' at the state from your last fetch. Since you haven't fetched since Monday 9am, your origin/main still reflects that state and you won't see the 5 commits pushed at 3pm or the new feature-login branch at all.

questions.question_text: You want to update all your remote-tracking branches and also remove any local remote-tracking branches that no longer exist on the remote servers. Write the single git command that accomplishes both of these tasks.
questions.explanation: The `git fetch --all --prune` command combines two flags: `--all` fetches from all configured remotes (not just the default origin), and `--prune` removes any remote-tracking branches that no longer exist on the remote. This is a best practice for keeping your local repository clean and synchronized with all remotes. The flags can also be written as `git fetch -a -p` or in reverse order.

questions.question_text: What happens when you run `git config --get` for a setting that doesn't exist?
options: It prints 'null' and returns exit code 0
It prints an error message and returns exit code 1
It prints nothing and returns exit code 1
It prints nothing and returns exit code 0
questions.explanation: When `git config --get` is used to query a non-existent setting, Git prints nothing to stdout and returns exit code 1. This behavior is particularly useful in shell scripts where you can check the exit code to determine if a setting exists, without having to parse any output.

questions.question_text:
You have 512 commits between a known good commit and a known bad commit. Approximately how many test iterations will git bisect require to find the first bad commit using binary search?

A. 512
B. 256
C. 9
D. 50
questions.explanation: Git bisect uses binary search, which has a time complexity of O(log₂ n). For 512 commits, log₂(512) = 9. This means approximately 9 iterations are needed because each step eliminates half of the remaining commits. This is the power of binary search - instead of potentially testing all 512 commits manually, you only need about 9 tests to find the culprit.

options: The `feature-login` branch checks only email and password
The `main` branch includes the `isActive` check
The `main` branch checks only email and password, while `feature-login` adds the `isActive` check
The conflict markers will be automatically removed after running `git commit`
questions.explanation: Since you're on `main` when merging, HEAD points to `main`. The code between `<<<<<<< HEAD` and `=======` (checking only email and password) is from `main`. The code between `=======` and `>>>>>>> feature-login` (which adds the `isActive` check) is from the incoming `feature-login` branch. Conflict markers must be manually removed—Git never removes them automatically.

questions.question_text: After resolving a merge conflict in a file called `config.js`, what are the correct steps to complete the merge? List the Git commands in order and explain why each step is necessary.
questions.correct_answer: 1. Edit config.js to resolve the conflict by choosing the correct code and removing ALL conflict markers (<<<<<<, ======, >>>>>>). 2. Run `git add config.js` to stage the resolved file. 3. Run `git commit` to complete the merge commit. The staging step tells Git you've resolved the conflict, and the commit finalizes the merge.
questions.explanation: Resolving a merge conflict requires three key steps: First, manually edit the conflicted file to keep the desired code and delete all conflict markers (`<<<<<<<`, `=======`, `>>>>>>>`). These markers are not valid code and must be completely removed. Second, use `git add` to stage the resolved file—this signals to Git that you've handled the conflict. Finally, run `git commit` to create the merge commit. If you skip the `git add` step, Git won't know the conflict is resolved. If you leave conflict markers in the file, your code will be broken.

questions.question_text: You accidentally committed 3 changes to the 'feature' branch but they should have been on the 'develop' branch. After using cherry-pick to copy the commits to 'develop', what command would you use to remove these 3 commits from the 'feature' branch?
options: A) git revert HEAD~3
B) git reset --soft HEAD~3
C) git reset --hard HEAD~3
D) git checkout HEAD~3
questions.explanation: The command 'git reset --hard HEAD~3' moves the branch pointer back by 3 commits and discards all changes in those commits from both the staging area and working directory. This effectively removes the commits from the branch. Option A (revert) creates new commits that undo changes rather than removing them. Option B (--soft) keeps changes staged. Option D doesn't modify the branch at all.

questions.question_text:
You have modified a file and accidentally staged it with `git add`. You want to unstage it but keep your modifications. Which command would achieve this?

```bash
# File was modified and staged
git add config.js

# Now you want to unstage it but keep changes
????
```
options: A. git restore config.js
B. git reset --hard HEAD config.js
C. git restore --staged config.js
D. git checkout -- config.js
questions.explanation: Option C (`git restore --staged config.js`) removes the file from the staging area while preserving working directory changes. Option A without `--staged` would discard working directory changes. Option B with `--hard` would also discard changes. Option D (`git checkout --`) is the old way to discard working directory changes, not to unstage files.

questions.question_text:
You have a repository with the following remote configuration:

```
origin  https://github.com/myorg/myrepo.git (fetch)
origin  https://github.com/myorg/myrepo.git (push)
```

Which command correctly switches this remote to use SSH?
options: A) git remote add origin git@github.com:myorg/myrepo.git
B) git remote set-url origin git@github.com:myorg/myrepo.git
C) git config --global remote.origin.url git@github.com:myorg/myrepo.git
D) git clone git@github.com:myorg/myrepo.git
questions.explanation: The `git remote set-url` command is used to change the URL of an existing remote. Option A would fail because 'origin' already exists. Option C would set a global config which isn't how remote URLs work. Option D would clone a new copy of the repository rather than modify the existing one. The correct syntax is `git remote set-url <remote-name> <new-url>`.

questions.question_text: You want to view only the diff of the last commit without any commit metadata (author, date, message). Which command should you use?
options: A. git show HEAD
B. git diff HEAD~1..HEAD
C. git show --stat HEAD
D. git log -p HEAD
questions.explanation: The command `git diff HEAD~1..HEAD` shows only the diff between the commit before HEAD (HEAD~1) and HEAD itself, without including commit metadata like author, date, or commit message. In contrast, `git show HEAD` includes metadata, and `git show --stat HEAD` shows file statistics along with metadata.

questions.question_text: Write a Git command that would show the changes made to a specific file called `src/main.py` in the last commit only.
questions.explanation: The `git show HEAD -- path/to/file` syntax allows you to filter the output of `git show` to display only the changes made to a specific file in the referenced commit. The double dash `--` separates the commit reference from the file path, which is especially important when file paths might be ambiguous.

questions.question_text: What is the relationship between `git diff --staged` and `git diff --cached`?
options: A. --staged shows more detailed output than --cached
B. --cached is deprecated and --staged is the replacement
C. They are synonyms and produce identical output
D. --staged compares with HEAD while --cached compares with the index
questions.explanation: The flags `--staged` and `--cached` are synonyms in Git and can be used interchangeably. Both commands show the diff between the staged (indexed) version of files and the last commit. Neither is deprecated, and they produce exactly the same output.

questions.question_text:
You want to add a shared logging library from GitHub directly to your project's `requirements.txt` without publishing to PyPI. Which syntax is correct?

```text
# Option A
logging_lib @ github.com/you/logging-lib.git@v0.1.0

# Option B
git+https://github.com/you/logging-lib.git@v0.1.0#egg=logging_lib

# Option C
https://github.com/you/logging-lib/v0.1.0

# Option D
github:you/logging-lib#v0.1.0
```
options: A) Option A
B) Option B
C) Option C
D) Option D
questions.explanation: The correct pip syntax for installing directly from a Git repository is `git+https://github.com/user/repo.git@tag#egg=package_name`. The `git+` prefix tells pip to use Git, `@v0.1.0` specifies the version/tag, and `#egg=logging_lib` provides the package name for pip's dependency resolution.

questions.question_text:
After running `git push -u origin feature-x` for a new local branch, which refs exist and what are their purposes?

```
refs/heads/feature-x
refs/remotes/origin/feature-x
```
options: A. Both are local branches where you can make commits
B. refs/heads/feature-x is for commits; refs/remotes/origin/feature-x is a read-only snapshot of the remote's state
C. refs/heads/feature-x is a backup; refs/remotes/origin/feature-x is the main working branch
D. Both point to the same commits and serve identical purposes
questions.explanation: These are two different types of refs. `refs/heads/feature-x` is your local branch where you make commits and do your work. `refs/remotes/origin/feature-x` is a remote-tracking branch, which is a local read-only cache that represents the state of the branch on the remote server. This remote-tracking branch updates when you run `git fetch`.

questions.question_text: You want to see which remote-tracking branches would be removed without actually deleting them. Which command should you use?
options: A. git remote prune origin --preview
B. git remote prune origin --dry-run
C. git remote prune origin --list
D. git remote prune origin --verbose
questions.explanation: The `--dry-run` flag is the correct option to preview what would be pruned without actually performing the deletion. This is a common Git pattern where `--dry-run` shows what an operation would do without executing it, allowing you to verify the action before committing to it.

questions.question_text:
A teammate tells you they just pushed important changes to the `feature-branch` on GitHub. You want to see their commits before merging. What should you do first?

```bash
# Current state: your origin/feature-branch might be outdated
```
options: A. Run `git log origin/feature-branch` immediately to see the commits
B. Run `git fetch` first, then `git log origin/feature-branch`
C. Run `git pull` to automatically merge their changes
D. Run `git push` to sync your branches
questions.explanation: Remote-tracking branches can be out of date. Since your teammate just pushed, your local `origin/feature-branch` doesn't yet reflect their changes. You need to run `git fetch` first to update your remote-tracking branch, then you can view the commits with `git log origin/feature-branch`. Option A would show stale data, option C would merge before you can review, and option D is for uploading your changes.

questions.question_text: After rebasing your feature branch onto `origin/main`, why is a force push (`git push --force`) typically required to update your remote branch?
questions.correct_answer: Because rebase rewrites your commits with new commit hashes, creating a divergent history from the remote branch. A regular push is rejected since the histories no longer match, so force push is needed to overwrite the remote branch with your rewritten local history.
questions.explanation: Rebasing doesn't just move commits — it creates entirely new commits with different SHA hashes, even if the content is the same. This means your local branch's history has diverged from the remote branch's history. Git's normal push safety checks prevent you from overwriting the remote with a divergent history, so you must use --force to explicitly tell Git to replace the remote branch with your rewritten version.

questions.question_text: Consider this scenario: You have a feature branch with 3 commits. You run `git rebase origin/main`. A teammate asks if they need to pull the latest `origin/main` after your rebase. What is the correct response?
options: A) Yes, because rebase updates origin/main with your changes
B) No, because rebase only modifies your local feature branch, not origin/main
C) Yes, because rebase merges your commits into origin/main
D) No, but only if you haven't force pushed yet
questions.explanation: Rebasing onto origin/main does not modify origin/main in any way. The operation only affects your local feature branch by replaying your commits on top of origin/main. Your teammate doesn't need to pull origin/main because of your rebase — origin/main remains exactly as it was. The only branch that changes is your feature branch, and after force pushing, your remote feature branch (not main).

questions.question_text:
After rebasing your local branch onto `origin/main`, what command should you use to update your remote branch, and why is `--force-with-lease` preferred over `--force`?

```bash
git rebase origin/main
git push ??? origin your-branch
```
questions.correct_answer: Use `git push --force-with-lease origin your-branch`. The `--force-with-lease` option is preferred because it's a safer alternative to `--force` - it will refuse to update the remote branch if someone else has pushed commits to it since you last fetched, preventing you from accidentally overwriting others' work.
questions.explanation: After rebasing, you must force push because rebase rewrites commit history, creating new commit hashes. Using `--force-with-lease` instead of `--force` adds a safety check that ensures you don't accidentally overwrite commits that others may have pushed to the remote branch while you were working locally.

questions.question_text: A developer notices they have a local branch called `feature-login` that no longer has a remote tracking reference. Which of the following is NOT a likely reason for this situation?
options: A. The branch was created for local experimentation and was never pushed
B. The remote branch was deleted after a pull request was merged
C. Git automatically removes tracking references after 30 days of inactivity
D. The developer intentionally created the branch for local-only configuration
questions.explanation: Git does not automatically remove tracking references based on inactivity. The other options are all valid reasons why a local branch might not have a remote tracking reference: it may have been created for experimentation (A), the remote branch may have been deleted after merging (B), or it may contain local-only customizations (D).

questions.question_text:
You want to see exactly which files were modified in your unpushed commits, along with insertion/deletion statistics. Which command should you use?

```bash
# Your local branch is 'feature' tracking 'origin/feature'
```
options: A. git diff origin/feature..HEAD
B. git log origin/feature..HEAD --stat
C. git log origin/feature..HEAD --oneline
D. git status origin/feature
questions.explanation: The `--stat` flag with `git log` shows a summary of files changed in each commit along with the number of insertions and deletions. Option A (`git diff`) would show the actual line-by-line changes, not a file summary. Option C would only show commit hashes and messages. Option D is not valid syntax for git status.

questions.question_text: Why did GitHub choose the term "Pull Request" for their code review feature?
questions.correct_answer: GitHub uses the term 'Pull Request' because the action being requested is to 'pull' the proposed changes into the target branch.
questions.explanation: The terminology reflects the underlying Git operation. When you create a Pull Request on GitHub, you're essentially asking the repository maintainer to 'pull' your changes into their branch. This aligns with the Git command 'git pull' which fetches and integrates changes. GitLab, in contrast, chose 'Merge Request' to emphasize that you're requesting your changes to be 'merged'.

questions.question_text: A developer who has only used GitHub joins a team that uses GitLab. They need to propose changes from their feature branch to the main branch. Which statement is correct about their workflow?
options: A. They must learn an entirely new review process since MRs work differently than PRs
B. They can use the same workflow they're familiar with - they just need to create a Merge Request instead of a Pull Request
C. GitLab doesn't support branch-based collaboration, only fork-based
D. They need to use command-line tools since GitLab doesn't have a web interface for code review
questions.explanation: Since Pull Requests (GitHub) and Merge Requests (GitLab) perform nearly identical functions, the developer can follow essentially the same workflow they're used to. They create a feature branch, push their changes, and then create a Merge Request (instead of a Pull Request) to propose merging their changes into the target branch. The review-and-merge flow is very similar across both platforms.

questions.question_text: You have an open PR from `feature-auth` to `main`. A reviewer requests changes, and you make the fixes locally. After committing and pushing to `feature-auth`, what is the recommended best practice?
options: A. Close the current PR and open a new one with the fixes
B. Force push to overwrite the entire branch history
C. Let reviewers know you've added new commits to address their feedback
D. Create a new branch and open a separate PR for the fixes
questions.explanation: When you push new commits to address reviewer feedback, the PR automatically updates. However, it's good practice to communicate with reviewers that you've made changes. This helps them know to re-review the PR and understand what was modified. There's no need to close the PR or create new branches for fixes to the same feature.

questions.question_text: You run `git pull --rebase` and it completes without errors. Your colleague then asks what happened to your original commits. Which statement is most accurate?
options: A. Your original commits remain unchanged with their original commit hashes
B. Your commits were replayed, creating new commits with new hashes on top of the fetched changes
C. Your commits were merged into a single squash commit
D. Your commits were temporarily stashed and then reapplied
questions.explanation: During a rebase, Git 'replays' your commits on top of the new base (the fetched remote changes). This process creates new commits with new SHA hashes, even though the changes in each commit remain the same. The original commits are replaced by these new commits in your branch history, resulting in a linear history without merge commits.

questions.question_text: After successfully rebasing your local branch onto `origin/main`, Git shows a message that your branch has diverged from the remote tracking branch. What is the correct action to take?
options: A. Run `git pull` to sync the branches
B. Run `git push --force-with-lease origin <branch-name>`
C. Run `git merge origin/<branch-name>` to reconcile the differences
D. Delete the local branch and checkout from remote again
questions.explanation: After a rebase, your local branch has rewritten commits with new SHA hashes, while the remote still has the old commits. Running `git pull` would merge the old commits back in and undo your rebase work. The correct approach is to force push with `--force-with-lease` to update the remote branch to match your rebased local branch. This flag is safer than `--force` because it checks if anyone else has pushed to the branch since you last fetched.

questions.question_text: Why does Git report that branches have 'diverged' after a successful rebase, even though the content is essentially the same?
questions.correct_answer: After a rebase, the local branch has new commits with different SHA hashes (rewritten on top of the target branch), while the remote branch still contains the original commits. Even though the code changes are the same, Git sees them as different commits because rebasing creates new commits with new identities, causing the histories to diverge.
questions.explanation: Rebasing doesn't just move commits—it recreates them with new parent commits, which means they get entirely new SHA hashes. Git tracks commits by their SHA hashes, so even if the actual code changes are identical, Git sees the rebased commits as completely different from the original commits on the remote. This is why the branches are reported as diverged.

questions.question_text: When you encounter a merge conflict after running `git stash apply`, what does the `<<<<<<< Updated upstream` marker represent?
options: A. Your uncommitted local changes that were stashed
B. The code currently in your working directory (committed version)
C. Changes from a remote branch you're pulling
D. The original version of the file before any changes
questions.explanation: When applying a stash, Git is essentially merging your stashed changes with your current working directory. The 'Updated upstream' marker indicates the code that was already in your working directory (the committed version from the repo), while 'Stashed changes' represents your local modifications that were stored in the stash.

questions.question_text: You have a local branch called `feature-x` that you want to track `origin/feature-x`. You are currently on the `main` branch. Which of the following commands will correctly set up the tracking relationship?
options: A. git branch -u origin/feature-x
B. git branch --set-upstream-to=origin/feature-x feature-x
C. git push -u origin feature-x
D. git config branch.feature-x.tracking origin/feature-x
questions.explanation: Option B is correct because `git branch --set-upstream-to=origin/feature-x feature-x` explicitly specifies both the remote tracking branch and the local branch name, which is required when you're not on the branch you want to configure. Option A would try to set tracking for the current branch (main), not feature-x. Option C pushes and sets upstream but requires you to be on the branch. Option D uses incorrect config syntax.

questions.question_text: A developer wants to contribute code to a public GitHub repository where they are not listed as a collaborator. Describe the correct workflow they must follow to submit their changes.
questions.correct_answer: The developer must: 1) Fork the repository to their own GitHub account, 2) Make changes in their forked copy, 3) Submit a Pull Request from their fork back to the original repository, 4) Wait for the repository owner to review and decide whether to merge the changes.
questions.explanation: This is the standard open-source contribution workflow on GitHub. Since non-collaborators cannot push directly to a public repository, they must create their own copy (fork), make changes there, and then request that the original maintainer pull their changes via a Pull Request. This protects the original repository while still enabling collaboration.

questions.question_text: Your organization has a public repository that should allow contributions from the community. A new team member suggests adding a setting to make the repository "read-only for external users." What is the correct response?
options: A) Direct them to Settings → Branches → Add branch protection rule
B) Explain that no action is needed because public repositories are already read-only for non-collaborators by default
C) Navigate to Settings → Collaborators and remove all external users
D) Change the repository visibility to private and enable public read access
questions.explanation: Public repositories on GitHub are inherently read-only for anyone who is not the owner, an invited collaborator, or an organization member with appropriate permissions. This is GitHub's default security model. No special settings need to be configured to prevent unauthorized users from pushing to a public repository - they simply cannot do so. External contributors must use the fork and pull request workflow.

questions.question_text:
What does the following command show?

```bash
git log origin/main..origin/feature --oneline
```
options: A. All commits that exist in both main and feature branches
B. Commits on the feature branch that are not in main
C. Commits on main that are not in the feature branch
D. The difference between the latest commits on both branches
questions.explanation: The double-dot notation `A..B` in git log shows commits that are reachable from B but not from A. So `origin/main..origin/feature` displays commits that exist on the feature branch but are not yet in main. This is useful for seeing what new work would be introduced if you merged feature into main. The reverse (`origin/feature..origin/main`) would show commits in main that aren't in feature.

questions.question_text: You want to see all files in your repository, including those nested in subdirectories, along with their blob hashes. Which command should you use?
options: git cat-file -p HEAD^{tree}
git ls-tree -r HEAD
git rev-parse HEAD^{tree}
git cat-file -p HEAD
questions.explanation: The `git ls-tree -r HEAD` command recursively lists all files in the tree. The `-r` flag enables recursive listing, which traverses into subdirectories. `git cat-file -p HEAD^{tree}` only shows the top-level contents without recursing into subtrees.

questions.question_text:
You need to configure a Docker build that clones a private GitHub repository. Which scopes should your Personal Access Token include at minimum?

A. `write:packages` and `delete_repo`
B. `read:packages` and `repo` (or `contents:read`)
C. `admin:org` and `gist`
D. `notifications` and `user:email`
options: A. write:packages and delete_repo
B. read:packages and repo (or contents:read)
C. admin:org and gist
D. notifications and user:email
questions.explanation: For cloning a private repository in a Docker build, you need `read:packages` to access any packages and `repo` (or the more limited `contents:read` for fine-grained tokens) to read repository contents. The principle of least privilege applies — you should only grant the minimum permissions necessary. Options A, C, and D include permissions that are either excessive (admin, delete) or unrelated to cloning repositories.

questions.question_text: You want to check if a specific branch named 'feature-auth' has an upstream configured. Which command would you use, and what does an empty output indicate?
questions.correct_answer:
git config --get branch.feature-auth.remote

An empty output (no output) indicates that the branch has no upstream configured.
questions.explanation: The command `git config --get branch.<branch-name>.remote` retrieves the remote associated with a specific branch. If the branch has an upstream, it returns the remote name (typically 'origin'). If there's no output, it means no upstream has been set for that branch. This is useful when you need to check a single branch without parsing through the output of all branches.

questions.question_text: A developer rebases their feature branch onto the updated main branch. The commit content, message, and author information remain identical, yet the commit hash changes. Explain why this happens.
questions.correct_answer: The commit hash changes because the parent commit is different after the rebase. Git calculates the commit hash using several inputs including the parent commit hash(es). When rebasing, the commit is replayed on top of a new base commit, which means it now has a different parent. Since the parent commit hash is part of the SHA-1 calculation, even though the content, message, and author are identical, the resulting commit hash will be completely different.
questions.explanation: This question tests understanding of how Git's hash calculation works and specifically why rebasing creates new commits. The key insight is that the parent commit reference is one of the inputs to the hash function, so any change in the commit's position in the history graph necessarily produces a new hash.

questions.question_text: What does the `--cached` flag do in the command `git rm -r --cached __pycache__/`?
options: A. Deletes the files from both Git and your local filesystem
B. Removes files from Git's tracking but keeps them on your local filesystem
C. Caches the files for faster access later
D. Permanently deletes the files from all Git history
questions.explanation: The `--cached` flag tells Git to remove files from the staging area and stop tracking them, but it preserves the actual files on your local filesystem. Without `--cached`, `git rm` would delete the files from both Git tracking AND your local disk.

questions.question_text:
Given the following `.gitignore` file, identify any syntax errors or issues:

```
# Dependencies
node_modules/, vendor/

# Logs
*.log
```
questions.correct_answer: The error is on line 2: multiple patterns are on the same line separated by a comma. Each pattern must be on its own line. The correct format would be to put `node_modules/` and `vendor/` on separate lines.
questions.explanation:
The `.gitignore` file requires exactly one pattern per line. Placing `node_modules/, vendor/` on a single line is incorrect because Git will interpret the entire string (including the comma) as a single pattern, which won't match what was intended. The fix is to put each pattern on its own line:
```
# Dependencies
node_modules/
vendor/
```

questions.question_text: You've created a `.gitignore` file in your project directory on macOS, but when you run `ls` in the terminal, you don't see it. What command should you use to verify the file exists?
options: A) ls -all
B) ls -la
C) ls --hidden
D) ls -visible
questions.explanation: Files starting with a dot (.) are hidden by default on Mac and Linux systems. The regular `ls` command won't display them. Using `ls -la` (or `ls -a`) shows all files including hidden ones. The `-l` flag provides the long format with additional details, and `-a` shows all files including those starting with a dot.

questions.question_text: A developer wants to ignore `.DS_Store` files across ALL of their Git repositories on their machine. Where should they configure this ignore rule?
options: A. In each repository's root .gitignore file
B. Inside the .git/config file
C. In ~/.gitignore_global
D. In the .git/info/exclude file of each repo
questions.explanation: The global gitignore file (~/.gitignore_global) is used to define ignore patterns that apply to all Git repositories on your machine. This is ideal for OS-specific files like .DS_Store (macOS) or Thumbs.db (Windows) that you never want to track in any project.

questions.question_text: Explain the difference between placing a `.gitignore` file at the repository root versus placing one in a subdirectory. When might you use a subdirectory `.gitignore`?
questions.correct_answer: A .gitignore at the repository root applies its rules to the entire repository, while a .gitignore in a subdirectory only applies to that folder and its subfolders. You might use a subdirectory .gitignore when a specific folder has unique ignore requirements, such as a build output directory that needs different rules than the rest of the project.
questions.explanation: Git allows multiple .gitignore files throughout a repository. The root .gitignore handles project-wide patterns, while subdirectory .gitignore files provide localized rules. This is useful for modular projects where different components have different ignore needs, or when you want to keep ignore rules close to the files they affect.

questions.question_text:
Which GitHub CLI command opens a specific Pull Request directly in your default web browser?

```bash
# Assume you want to view PR #42
```
options: A. gh pr open 42
B. gh pr view 42 --web
C. gh pr browse 42
D. gh pr show 42 --browser
questions.explanation: The correct command is `gh pr view <pr_number> --web`. The `gh pr view` command displays PR details, and the `--web` flag tells the CLI to open the PR in your default web browser instead of showing it in the terminal.

questions.question_text: A team member mentions PR #157 in a commit message. Describe two different methods you could use to navigate to this Pull Request on GitHub without manually typing the full URL.
questions.correct_answer: Method 1: Click on the #157 reference in the commit message or any issue/PR where it's mentioned - GitHub automatically converts PR/issue numbers prefixed with # into clickable links. Method 2: Navigate to the repository's Pull Requests tab, then search or scroll to find PR #157 and click on it. Alternative methods include: using GitHub CLI with 'gh pr view 157 --web', or checking email notifications if you're subscribed to the repository.
questions.explanation: GitHub provides multiple convenient ways to access PRs. The # notation (like #157) is automatically converted to clickable links throughout the GitHub interface, making cross-referencing easy. The Pull Requests tab provides a searchable list of all PRs, and the GitHub CLI offers command-line access with browser integration.

questions.question_text:
A developer's GitHub Actions workflow has failed. Which steps should they take to debug the issue?

Arrange the following actions in the correct order:
1. Expand different steps in the job
2. Click on the Actions tab
3. Click on the failed workflow run
4. View logs and error messages
questions.explanation: The correct debugging sequence is: First, click on the Actions tab to access workflows (2), then click on the specific failed workflow run (3), then expand different steps within the job (1), and finally view the logs and error messages to identify the issue (4). This systematic approach helps developers efficiently locate and diagnose CI/CD failures.

questions.question_text: What is the key difference between `git revert` and `git rebase` when dealing with a commit that contains sensitive information like API keys?
options: A) git revert removes the commit from history, while git rebase keeps it
B) git revert creates a new commit that undoes changes but keeps the original commit in history, while git rebase can completely remove the commit from history
C) git revert is faster than git rebase for security scenarios
D) git revert and git rebase produce identical results for security purposes
questions.explanation: git revert creates a NEW commit that undoes the changes from a previous commit, but the original commit (containing the sensitive credentials) remains visible in the repository history. Anyone with access to the repo can still view the leaked information by browsing commit history. git rebase -i, on the other hand, rewrites history and can completely remove the problematic commit, making it as if it never existed. For security incidents involving leaked credentials, rewriting history with rebase is essential.

questions.question_text: After using `git rebase -i` to remove a commit containing leaked credentials from your local repository, what additional step is required to update the remote repository?
options: A) git push origin main
B) git push --force-with-lease
C) git pull --rebase
D) git merge --squash
questions.explanation: After rewriting history with git rebase, your local branch has diverged from the remote branch. A regular `git push` will be rejected because Git detects the histories don't match. You must use `git push --force-with-lease` to overwrite the remote history with your rewritten local history. The `--force-with-lease` option is preferred over `--force` because it provides a safety check - it will fail if someone else has pushed changes to the remote branch since you last fetched, preventing you from accidentally overwriting their work.

questions.question_text: Why must wildcards be quoted when using `git restore` with patterns like `*.txt`?
options: A) Git does not support wildcard characters without quotes
B) The shell expands unquoted globs before Git receives them
C) Quotes make the command run faster
D) Git requires single quotes for all file arguments
questions.explanation: Without quotes, the shell (bash, zsh, etc.) performs glob expansion before passing arguments to Git. This means Git receives a list of already-matched filenames instead of the pattern itself. If no files match the pattern in the current directory, the shell may pass the literal glob or throw an error, causing unexpected behavior.

questions.question_text:
You have the following files in your Git repository:
- `main.py` (tracked, with uncommitted changes)
- `config.json` (tracked, staged for commit)
- `debug.log` (untracked file)
- `.env` (ignored via .gitignore)

After running `git reset --hard HEAD`, which files will still exist in your working directory?
options: A) Only main.py and config.json (restored to last commit)
B) main.py, config.json (restored), debug.log, and .env
C) Only main.py and config.json with their current changes preserved
D) None - all files are deleted
questions.explanation: git reset --hard only affects TRACKED files - it resets them to match the specified commit and discards any staged or unstaged changes. However, it does NOT delete untracked files (debug.log) or ignored files (.env). These remain untouched in your working directory. This is a common misconception - many developers expect reset --hard to give them a completely clean directory, but it only operates on files Git is tracking.

questions.question_text: After completing a git rebase where you skipped a commit using `git rebase --skip`, what happens to that skipped commit?
options: A. The commit is permanently deleted from the repository
B. The commit still exists in Git's history and can be found via reflog
C. The commit is automatically merged into the next rebase operation
D. The commit is moved to a special 'skipped' branch
questions.explanation: When you skip a commit during rebase, it is excluded from the rebased branch but is not deleted from Git's object database. The commit still exists and can be located using `git reflog`, which tracks all reference updates including commits that are no longer part of any branch. This allows you to recover the commit later via cherry-pick if needed.

questions.question_text:
You are in the middle of a rebase and encounter a complex merge conflict. You realize you need more time to understand the codebase before resolving it. What is the recommended approach?

```bash
# Current state: rebase in progress with unresolved conflict
```
options: A. Use `git rebase --skip` to skip the commit and cherry-pick it later
B. Use `git rebase --abort` to cancel the rebase and return later when ready
C. Force push the incomplete changes with `git push --force`
D. Use `git stash` to save the conflict state for later
questions.explanation: When you need more time to resolve a conflict properly, the recommended approach is to use `git rebase --abort`. This cancels the rebase operation entirely and returns your branch to its state before the rebase began. Using `--skip` is meant for commits you genuinely don't want, not for commits you want but need time to handle. Aborting allows you to come back later and attempt the rebase again with a fresh start.

questions.question_text:
When a merge conflict occurs during a git rebase, what information does Git provide to help you identify the problem?

```
Rebasing (3/5)
error: could not apply abc1234... Your commit message here
```
options: A. Only the file names with conflicts
B. The commit hash and commit message of the conflicting commit
C. Only the line numbers where conflicts occurred
D. The name of the person who made the conflicting changes
questions.explanation: When a conflict occurs during rebase, Git stops and displays the commit hash (abc1234) and the commit message of the specific commit that caused the conflict. This is helpful because you know exactly which of your changes is conflicting with the target branch, making it easier to understand the context of the conflict.

questions.question_text: Explain what happens after you resolve a conflict during a git rebase and run `git rebase --continue`. What output would you expect to see if you were on commit 3 of 5 when the conflict occurred?
questions.correct_answer: After resolving the conflict and running `git rebase --continue`, Git picks up where it left off and continues replaying the remaining commits. You would see output showing the progress resuming from the next commit, such as `Rebasing (4/5)` followed by `Rebasing (5/5)`, and finally a success message like `Successfully rebased and updated refs/heads/your-branch.`
questions.explanation: Git rebase processes commits sequentially. When a conflict occurs, Git pauses and waits for you to resolve it. After resolution and continuing, Git resumes from the next commit in the sequence. Since the conflict was on commit 3, Git would proceed to replay commits 4 and 5, showing the progress output for each, and then display the success message indicating the rebase completed and the branch reference was updated.

questions.question_text:
You're rebasing a feature branch onto main. Your branch history shows:
1. Commit A: Modified `config.yaml` to add a new setting
2. Commit B: Renamed `config.yaml` to `config-old.yaml`
3. Commit C: Created new `config.yaml` with different content

Meanwhile, main also modified the original `config.yaml`. During rebase, at which commit will you most likely encounter a merge conflict?
options: A. Commit C, because it creates a new file
B. Commit B, because it renames the file
C. Commit A, because Git replays commits in order and this commit modified the same file as main
D. No conflict will occur because the file was renamed
questions.explanation: During rebase, Git replays commits in order. When it reaches Commit A, it tries to apply your changes to `config.yaml` on top of main's version of that file—causing a conflict. The fact that you later renamed the file (Commit B) doesn't matter yet because that commit hasn't been applied. This is a common source of confusion: the conflict reflects the state at that point in your commit history, not your branch's final state.

questions.question_text: You're in the middle of implementing a feature and have uncommitted changes, but you need to rebase your branch onto the latest main. You want to preserve your work without creating a permanent commit. Which sequence of commands should you use?
options: A. git checkout -- . && git rebase -i origin/main
B. git reset --hard && git rebase -i origin/main
C. git stash && git rebase -i origin/main && git stash pop
D. git add . && git rebase -i origin/main
questions.explanation: Using git stash temporarily saves your uncommitted changes, allowing you to perform the rebase with a clean working directory. After the rebase completes, git stash pop restores your changes. Options A and B would discard your changes permanently, and option D alone won't work because git add only stages changes—you still need to commit or stash them.

questions.question_text:
You want to experiment with an interactive rebase on your feature branch without risking your original work. Which approach correctly simulates a "dry run" for interactive rebase?

```bash
# Option A
git rebase -i --dry-run origin/main

# Option B
git checkout -b test-rebase feature-branch
git rebase -i origin/main

# Option C
git rebase -i --preview origin/main

# Option D
git stash
git rebase -i origin/main
```
options: A) Option A - using the --dry-run flag
B) Option B - creating a temporary branch first
C) Option C - using the --preview flag
D) Option D - stashing changes before rebasing
questions.explanation: Git rebase has no built-in --dry-run or --preview flag. The correct approach is to create a temporary branch (test-rebase) from your feature branch, then perform the interactive rebase on that temporary branch. If something goes wrong or you don't like the result, you can simply delete the test branch and your original feature branch remains untouched.

questions.question_text: You are in the middle of an interactive rebase and encounter a merge conflict that you realize will require significant rework of your approach. You decide you want to completely abandon the rebase and restore your branch to its exact state before you started. What is the correct command to accomplish this?
questions.explanation: The command `git rebase --abort` will stop the current rebase operation and restore your branch to its state before the rebase started. This is the safe way to back out of a rebase that's in progress, whether you've encountered conflicts or simply changed your mind about the operation. This is different from `git rebase --skip` (which skips the current commit) or `git rebase --continue` (which proceeds after resolving conflicts).

questions.question_text: You are rebasing your feature branch onto main. Your branch has an `if use_legacy_agent:` conditional structure, while main has added new parameters (`intra_run_history_token_limit` and `tokenizer_model_name`) to the `CueAgent.create()` call. What is the correct approach to resolve this conflict by combining both changes?
options: Keep only your branch's changes and discard main's new parameters
Keep only main's changes and discard your conditional structure
Preserve your conditional structure and incorporate main's new parameters into the appropriate code block
Create a new branch and cherry-pick individual commits to avoid the conflict
questions.explanation: When combining code changes during conflict resolution, you should preserve the structural changes from your branch (the if/else conditional) while incorporating the functional updates from main (the new parameters). This ensures both sets of changes are retained and work together correctly. Simply choosing one side would lose important functionality.

questions.question_text:
Given the following branch structure:

```
main:        A -- B -- C
your-branch:          \ -- D -- E
```

After rebasing `your-branch` onto `main`, what happens to commits D and E?
options: A. D and E remain exactly the same with their original commit hashes
B. D and E are deleted and replaced with a single squashed commit
C. D and E are replayed as D' and E' with new commit hashes
D. D and E are moved into a merge commit M
questions.explanation: When you rebase, Git replays your commits on top of the target branch. The commits contain the same changes, but they get new commit hashes (D becomes D', E becomes E') because their parent commits have changed. The original commits are not preserved with their original hashes—they are essentially recreated with new hashes while maintaining the same changes.

questions.question_text: Explain why some developers prefer using git rebase over git merge for feature branches, and describe what 'linear history' means in this context.
questions.correct_answer: Developers prefer git rebase for feature branches because it keeps the commit history clean and linear by not creating extra merge commits. Linear history means the commits appear in a single straight line of progression (A -> B -> C -> D' -> E') rather than having branching and merging points. This makes the history easier to read, understand, and navigate when reviewing changes or debugging issues.
questions.explanation: Git rebase replays commits without creating merge commits, resulting in a cleaner, linear history. A linear history shows commits in a single progression line rather than showing branch divergence and merge points. This is particularly valuable for feature branches that will be integrated into main, as it avoids cluttering the main branch history with numerous merge commits and makes tools like git log and git bisect easier to use.

questions.question_text: You are rebasing a feature branch with 5 commits onto main. Git encounters a conflict on the 3rd commit. After resolving it and continuing, another conflict occurs on the 4th commit. You realize the 4th commit is no longer relevant and want to discard it entirely. What command should you use?
options: A. git rebase --abort
B. git reset --hard HEAD
C. git rebase --skip
D. git checkout --theirs .
questions.explanation: 'git rebase --skip' will skip the current conflicting commit entirely and move on to replay the next commit. This is appropriate when you've determined a commit is no longer needed. 'git rebase --abort' would cancel the entire rebase and lose your previous conflict resolutions. 'git reset --hard HEAD' doesn't help in this context, and 'git checkout --theirs' would accept their changes but still require you to continue the rebase.

questions.question_text: Explain what happens to your branch when you run `git rebase --abort` after encountering multiple conflicts during a rebase operation. What state will your branch be in?
questions.correct_answer: Running git rebase --abort cancels the entire rebase operation and restores your branch to its original state before the rebase started. All commits remain exactly as they were, and any conflict resolutions you made during the partial rebase are discarded.
questions.explanation: The --abort flag is a safety mechanism that completely undoes the rebase attempt. Git keeps track of the original branch state when you start a rebase, allowing you to safely abandon the operation if things go wrong. This is particularly useful when you encounter complex conflicts that you're not ready to resolve, or when you realize the rebase strategy isn't appropriate for your situation.

questions.question_text: During a rebase, you encounter conflicts in two files: `justfile` and `cue_eval/runner.py`. What is the correct sequence of steps to resolve and continue the rebase?
options: A. Run `git rebase --continue`, then resolve conflicts, then run `git add`
B. Resolve conflicts in both files, run `git commit`, then run `git rebase --continue`
C. Resolve conflicts in both files, run `git add justfile cue_eval/runner.py`, then run `git rebase --continue`
D. Run `git rebase --skip` to bypass the conflicts
questions.explanation: The correct workflow is to first resolve all conflicts in the affected files, then stage the resolved files using `git add`, and finally run `git rebase --continue`. You don't need to run `git commit` separately because `git rebase --continue` handles the commit as part of the rebase process.

questions.question_text: You rebased your feature branch onto the latest main branch and are ready to push. A colleague mentions they pushed a small fix to your feature branch while you were rebasing. What happens if you run `git push --force-with-lease`?
options: A) The push succeeds and your colleague's commit is preserved on top of your rebased commits
B) The push succeeds and your colleague's commit is silently overwritten
C) The push fails because the remote branch has changed since you last fetched
D) Git automatically merges your colleague's commit into your rebased branch
questions.explanation: The `--force-with-lease` flag includes a safety check that verifies the remote branch is at the commit you expect (based on your last fetch). Since your colleague pushed a new commit after you last fetched, the remote branch has moved. Git will reject the push and warn you, preventing you from accidentally overwriting your colleague's work. This is the key advantage over plain `--force`.

questions.question_text: When you run `git push`, why does Git also send tree objects along with commit objects?
questions.correct_answer: Git must send tree objects because commits reference (point to) root tree objects. Without the tree objects (and the blobs/subtrees they reference), the remote would have commits pointing to nothing — the commit would be incomplete and unusable since Git couldn't reconstruct the repository state at that commit.
questions.explanation: A commit is essentially a pointer to a root tree that represents the state of the repository at that point in time. The tree objects contain references to blobs (file contents) and subtrees (subdirectories). If Git only sent the commit without its referenced tree structure, the remote repository would have an incomplete and broken commit that couldn't be checked out or used.

questions.question_text:
You've cloned a repository and want to review your colleague's recent commits on the remote `main` branch before deciding whether to integrate them. After running the appropriate command to download the remote changes without modifying your working directory, which commands could you use to inspect those changes?

```bash
# After downloading remote changes safely...
```
options: git status and git log
git log HEAD..origin/main and git diff HEAD origin/main
git show origin/main and git remote -v
git branch -a and git checkout origin/main
questions.explanation: After running `git fetch`, the remote changes are stored in `origin/main` but not merged into your local branch. To inspect these changes: `git log HEAD..origin/main` shows the list of commits that exist on the remote but not in your local HEAD, and `git diff HEAD origin/main` shows the actual code differences between your current state and the remote branch. The other options either don't show the comparison between local and remote states or are not designed for this purpose.

questions.question_text: You are currently on the `main` branch and run `git pull`. Which of the following best describes what happens?
options: A. Only the main branch is fetched and merged
B. All remote branches are fetched, but only origin/main is merged into your local main
C. All remote branches are fetched and merged into their corresponding local branches
D. Only origin/main is fetched and no merge occurs
questions.explanation: When you run `git pull`, Git performs two operations: first, it fetches updates for ALL remote branches (updating all origin/* references), then it ONLY merges the remote tracking branch that corresponds to your current branch. So while all remote branches are downloaded, only origin/main gets merged into your local main branch.

questions.question_text:
You have a Git repository with the following remotes configured:

```bash
$ git remote -v
origin    https://github.com/user/repo.git (fetch)
origin    https://github.com/user/repo.git (push)
upstream  https://github.com/original/repo.git (fetch)
upstream  https://github.com/original/repo.git (push)
```

Which command should you use to ensure you fetch specifically from the `upstream` remote?
options: A. git fetch
B. git fetch origin
C. git fetch upstream
D. git pull upstream
questions.explanation: `git fetch upstream` explicitly specifies the upstream remote to fetch from. When you have multiple remotes configured, using `git fetch` without arguments would fetch from the default remote (usually origin), not upstream. Option D (`git pull`) would fetch AND merge, which is more than what was asked.

questions.question_text:
Given the following scenario, what remotes will be updated?

You have two remotes configured:
- `origin` (your fork)
- `upstream` (the original repository)

You run:
```bash
git fetch
```
options: A. Both `origin` and `upstream` will be fetched
B. Only `origin` will be fetched
C. Only `upstream` will be fetched
D. Neither will be fetched; you must specify a remote explicitly
questions.explanation: When you run `git fetch` without any arguments, Git fetches from the default remote, which is typically `origin`. To fetch from all configured remotes including `upstream`, you would need to use `git fetch --all` or explicitly specify `git fetch upstream`.

questions.question_text: In what situation would `git fetch` and `git fetch --all` produce identical results?
questions.correct_answer: When you only have one remote configured (typically just `origin`), both commands will produce the same result since there is only one remote to fetch from.
questions.explanation: The `--all` flag tells Git to fetch from all configured remotes. If your repository only has a single remote (like `origin`), then fetching from 'all remotes' is equivalent to fetching from just that one remote. The practical difference only emerges when you have multiple remotes configured.

questions.question_text: You are currently working on a feature branch called `feature/login` and want to see the latest changes that your teammates have pushed to the remote repository. Do you need to switch to the `main` branch before running `git fetch origin`?
options: A. Yes, you must always be on main to fetch from origin
B. Yes, because fetch only updates the currently checked out branch
C. No, git fetch can be run from any branch without negative side effects
D. No, but the fetch will only download changes related to your current branch
questions.explanation: You can run `git fetch origin` from any branch—it doesn't matter which one is checked out. The fetch command downloads commits for all branches from the remote into your local tracking references (like `origin/main`, `origin/feature/login`, etc.) without modifying your local branches or working directory. This makes it a safe operation to run at any time, regardless of your current branch.

questions.question_text: Explain why `git fetch origin` is considered a "safe" operation that can be run from any branch without affecting your current work.
